title: Embeddings & Representation Learning
summary: Mapping discrete or high-dimensional inputs to dense learned vector spaces where geometry encodes meaning. The quality of embedding geometry determines what downstream models can learn. Foundational to transformers, retrieval, and knowledge graphs.
key_insights: Embedding = lookup table $W_{\text{embed}} \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$. Token ID → row of $W$. NOT a matrix multiply, just indexing.
Geometry IS meaning: similar items should have similar vectors. $\cos(E_a, E_b)$ measures semantic similarity.
Word2Vec insight: $\vec{\text{king}} - \vec{\text{man}} + \vec{\text{woman}} \approx \vec{\text{queen}}$. Linear algebra in embedding space = semantic algebra.
Embedding dimension $d$ = capacity. Too small: can't separate concepts. Too large: waste + overfitting.
In transformers, embedding and un-embedding matrices are often TIED: $W_{\text{unembed}} = W_{\text{embed}}^\top$. Reduces params by $V \times d$.
Embeddings are learned end-to-end via backprop. No separate training step needed (unlike Word2Vec).

mathematical_core.output_projection: Logits $= h \cdot W_{\text{unembed}}$ where $W_{\text{unembed}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times V}$. If tied: $W_{\text{unembed}} = W_{\text{embed}}^\top$.
mathematical_core.param_count: $V \times d_{\text{model}}$. For GPT-2: $50257 \times 768 \approx 38.6M$ params just for embeddings.

derivations.title: Why embeddings work — distributional hypothesis

steps.name: Distributional hypothesis
steps.math: Words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings. Training on context prediction forces similar-context words to have similar embeddings.

steps.name: Gradient flow through embedding
steps.math: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{\text{embed}}[k]} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial E_k}$ — only the row for the current token gets updated per step. Sparse gradient.

steps.name: Weight tying saves parameters
steps.math: Without tying: $V \times d$ (embed) + $d \times V$ (unembed) = $2Vd$. With tying: $Vd$. 50% savings. Also acts as regularizer — forces input/output spaces to be coherent.

derivations.consequence: Embeddings learn from co-occurrence patterns. Weight tying halves params and improves generalization by forcing input and output representations to share structure.

derivations.title: Embedding norm and its role

steps.name: After training
steps.math: Frequent tokens develop larger norms (more gradient updates). $\|E_{\text{frequent}}\| > \|E_{\text{rare}}\|$. This is a signal, not a bug.

steps.name: Interaction with PE
steps.math: $X = E + \text{PE}$. If $\|\text{PE}\| \gg \|E\|$: position dominates content. Some models scale: $X = \sqrt{d} \cdot E + \text{PE}$.

derivations.consequence: Embedding norm encodes token frequency/importance. Balance with PE magnitude matters for early training dynamics.

geometric_properties: Embedding space should NOT collapse — all vectors pointing in the same direction = no information
Monitor: average pairwise cosine similarity. Should be low (diverse) not high (collapsed).
Analogy structure: $\vec{a} - \vec{b} + \vec{c} \approx \vec{d}$ works when relationships are linear in embedding space
statistical_properties: Embedding gradient is sparse: only the accessed row gets updated. Effective learning rate higher for frequent tokens.
Rare tokens have undertrained embeddings — may need special treatment (subword tokenization helps).
Monitor embedding drift: $\|E_t - E_{t-1}\|$ per epoch. Should stabilize.
numerical_properties: Embedding lookup: $O(d)$ per token (copy one row). Negligible compute, significant memory.
Memory: $V \times d \times 4$ bytes (float32). GPT-2: $\approx 154$MB just for embeddings.
Gradient accumulation for rare tokens: may need higher learning rate or separate optimizer.

related: FOUNDATIONS.INDUCTIVE_BIAS — embedding geometry is learned structure

notes: Graph node embeddings as K/V in cross-attention connects to knowledge graph + transformer hybrid designs.
